sentences.text: The languages that were used in-- in Torun, and not only in Torun, the whole Carpathian, in-- in the Jewish homes was Yiddish.
The second language was, of course, Ruthenian because the majority of the people, like I said, 90% of the people were Ruthenian.
They call themselves, at that time, small Russians.
I don't know how-- how-- now it is incorporated into the Ukraine.
And-- but it-- the-- the language, the small Russian, the way it was spoken in the Carpathian, is a slight bit bit different than the real Ukraine, because I was in the Ukraine later.
But all Slavic languages, if you know one, you can understand the rest of them.
Now, there are also, of course, the official language was Czech in-- in-- in the offices.
You were able to, in the Carpathia, you were able to use those two languages, your Ruthenian and-- and Czech.
And we-- we-- my-- my oldest brothers, both Moshe and Eliezer, since they-- and my father-- since they were under Austria-Hungary, they knew Hungarian.
But I didn't hear the language, and they didn't speak it at home.
They spoke it until 1900 and-- for, no, more than 14.
You know, the war ended in '18. 1914 it broke out.
I think in one year, in 1917, I think, I remember they were talking in the house that my oldest brother was drafted in the Austria-Hungarian, and my father was drafted in it.
So they were-- they-- they knew a little bit Hungarian, but I didn't know Hungarian.
All we know was mother tongue.
My mother tongue is Yiddish, and-- and what we learned in Czech school, the-- the Czech and Ukrainian.
And of course, since I went to Hebrew school in Cheder that-- that you learn the Hebrew.
Now, you-- you wanted to ask me about the warriors.
I have to start with 1938. 1938, the Hebrew equivalent is [HEBREW].
And 5,298, that was the year equivalent to-- to 1938.
In Hebrew [HEBREW] spelled tirzah.
And tirzah in Hebrew translates murder.
And that's when the murders started. 1938 was the Kristallnacht.
In November '38, that was when the first murder started, murdering the Jews.

And sometimes in June, July of 1938, the Munich-- infamous Munich Pact or dictate where Hitler dictated to the-- to the Allies, Chamberlain and-- and-- and Daladier, I think, was the name of the French prime minister that they-- they agreed to dismantle Czechoslovakia to give back the Sudeten area to Germany.
From that time till March 15, 1939, there was a six-month window of opportunity maybe to get out.

Because when-- when the English and the French dictated for the Czech government to surrender part of-- of the Sudeten gebeit, Sudetenland, and part of-- part of it to-- to see part of it to-- to Hungary, we were still under Czech control.
About from-- from October to March 15, on March 15, 1939, the-- the Germans moved in to Prague, occupied Czechoslovakia completely.
The Slovaks declared [? in ?] their independence.
The Ruthenians, in our area, declared also independence, their own independence, which was very, very scary for us.
But it lasted only about 48 hours.

And it collapsed when Hungary, according to the Encyclopedia Britannica, it is-- that-- that-- it was [? where ?] the acquiescence of Hitler to move into Carpathia, but I don't think that this is quite correct.
I think they did it on their own.
Because Hitler Germany supported the Carpathian independence.
And they declared their independence, and-- and they declared Khust as their capital city.
But they hadn't had enough power, Army, the Hungarians overrun them on March 15.
And so all of a sudden, we came-- became Hungarian citizens.
In a way, at that time, that-- that was for us a-- a celebration.
Because we feared very much of-- about the Ukrainian independence because we knew that this is supported by-- by Germany.

And besides that, we had a bad-- we have a bad history with-- with Ukrainian independence that goes back to Bogdan Khmelnitsky in 1648 and '49 and also to the [INAUDIBLE] era of 1917 where tens of villages were massacred in the-- in the Ukraine when they-- they wanted to become independent from-- from Russia.
So when the Hungarians came in, we were in a very-- a little bit relieved, at least here is a viable government, but it didn't last long.
They, Hungary, became an ally of-- of Germany, and Germany was pressing on them to-- to deal with the-- with the Jewish question, with the Jews.
So after about five, six months, after the Hungarians occupied this area, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] they started-- new edicts started coming out.
And the first one that-- that-- the first laws that they passed was that Jews are not allowed to take part to-- in the lumber industry, to-- to deal with the lumber industry.
And that hit especially us in the Carpathian, in-- because most of us were-- were sustaining ourselves with that industry.
That was the only industry.
Of course, the Hungarians grabbed this part, because this is a-- a-- a area that has a lot of lumber, which they didn't have in their own native-- native land.
So that's when it started in '39.
But somehow, we coped with it.
We coped-- The-- the Jews were not allowed to be in-- in-- in the lumber business.
But we-- we tried to-- to-- to find loopholes in the law.
And one of the loopholes was that if you found what we call at that time [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] is literally a front, a person.
It-- it was a-- it had to be a Gentile that took over-- performed the business, became the be official owner, and the real owners were getting some-- some part of it.
And that went on-- that went on for-- for another-- another year or so.
Since we did business, my father did business under the Czechs yet with a-- a firm from Budapest, and that-- one of their representatives, their firm's, the firm's name was [?
Halos. ?] They were-- they were-- that was a Jewish firm in Budapest, but they employed a German by the name of Schrammel.
And, you know, this business was you bought something that will be delivered in a half a year, three quarters of a year.
So they-- the way the work progressed, their representative would tell them that so-- so much work is done, and that's how they would pay.
They wouldn't pay at-- at-- up front.
They would pay up front a certain amount but not-- not what it costs to-- to-- to cut it and to bring it out.
So this guy, Schrammel, my father knew him and had connections with him, so he became, all of a sudden, he became the owner.
And he-- he was the [NON-ENGLISH], and we were able to more or less breathe.
That was in '39.
In '40, it started getting worse.
In-- in September, September the 1st, which was, I think, on a-- on a high holiday of the Rosh Hashanah, 1940.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And September the 1st, 1940, the Germans attacked Poland.
They had made a secret agreement with the Soviet Union with the other monster, Stalin.
That, of course, that was a secret.
Nobody knew about it.
And all of a sudden, Poland was overrun by Germany.
And under the secret agreement between Poland and Russia, the Russians got the part of Ukraine and the Poles-- and the Germans got the rest of-- of Poland.
So all of a sudden, in 1940, we became neighbors to the Soviet Union.
Because they came all the way to-- to Torun in 1940.
In a-- in a way-- in a way we were-- we were happy that at least that part of the Polish jury was more or less-- or less safe from physical destruction.
Because that-- that consisted of the cities, large cities like Lvov, Lemburg, Stryi, [INAUDIBLE]-- those were large Jewish communities that fell under the Soviet-- under the Soviet rule.
So the Germans and the-- and the Russians divided up Poland.
And we became-- Torun became the border.
So the Hungarians started sending in troops into the-- into the-- to the city in Torun by the border, was choked full of Hungarian soldiers.
They took away the-- the synagogue.
They took away the Hebrew school, and the-- the-- the houses.
If you had an extra room or two, if you had four rooms, they would take two rooms.
They came-- while they became the-- the neighbors of the Soviet Union.
So they started-- that was-- started building fortifications in 1940.

And at that time already, the Hungarian government would organize Jew-- male Jews into labor camps, labor camps that were called [INAUDIBLE], that were organized on a military, military form like 200 and 220 a company.
And the ages would be-- but that would be just males.
The ages would be between 20 and 40 or between 20 and 35.
And at the beginning-- at the beginning they would even supply those-- those with uniforms, those slave laborers.
The only thing was that the Hungarians had on their uniforms the buttons on the-- on-- had the Hungarian crown so that was treif.
The Jews shouldn't wear the Hungarian crown.
So they would have to take those-- those buttons off and put on regular buttons.
But they had-- they had at least uniforms.
That was in 1940.
It started getting very, very, very tight.
They-- they would call-- call in.
It-- it-- it was set in the beginning, call in for three months, for four months, you know.
But at least they left the old, and they left the women still in-- in their home.
And that went on till 1941.
In '41, on August the-- the-- the 2nd to the 3rd, which happened to fell on the-- not a holiday, the commemoration of the Tisha B'Av fast, which fell on a Saturday.
And keeping with Jewish tradition, when a fast day falls on a Saturday, it is postponed a day later and it's called-- it was called Tisha B'Av [? netray ?], postponed.
And this was exactly August the 2nd, 1941.
So it was postponed to August the 3rd.
We hadn't-- we didn't have this-- the-- the slightest idea that this will be the last night that we slept in our house.

I forgot to mention that the commander of the Hungarian garrison that was the commander in Torun-- already they were in there for months-- he was a colonel, that was the highest ranking officer there.
He took two rooms out of our house.
And he lived in our-- in our house.
And I should-- I should mention, he happened to be friendly.
We gave him those-- those two rooms.
And across the street from our house was the building of the Hebrew school.
That's where they had their headquarters.
And you know, in the summertime, the windows were open in our house and the windows were open at school.
And we could hear this-- this commander talking to the officers, Hungarian officers, that getting prepared because we are going to be attacking the Soviet Union and pep talks to the soldiers.
Of course, I didn't understand a word of Hungarian, but my oldest brother that spoke Hungarian was in the house.
And-- and he-- he would explain what-- we overheard what they were saying.
Anyway, this-- this commander, that-- that colonel, came one day and told my father, and he said, it was-- it was in more less in a friendly, secretive mood, he wants to do us a favor.
And he said, it would be advisable if you would move away from here.
Move 20, 30, 40 kilometer-- kilometers down.
Because this is-- this is a war zone, a war zone here.
Of course, we didn't know.
We didn't know what he meant, if he wants to take us-- take away the house from us there because he-- he needs it or he is really trying to do us a favor.
Anyway, the-- the-- the movement, it was restricted because of all the military that were there.
And we-- my father asked him to-- to give us a-- a written paper that we will-- we will move.
He had a sister that lived 70 kilometers deeper, close to the city of Khust.
We will take some belongings and send it over there.
But we had to have a permit.
He gave us a permit.
And-- and-- the-- the other Jews saw what happened that-- that we are sending some-- some stuff away from the house.
And a panic broke out.

But no-- nobody-- it-- it-- it's for your-- for your own good, but no, it was-- nobody is-- wants to leave a house where-- that he lives in and-- and built and everything for so many years, just-- just to leave it and leave and go.
But anyway, we did some stuff, clothing, we send out clothing, some-- some of-- of-- jewelry that-- that my mother had and my father had.
And my older brother, Tsvi, I forgot to mention, by that time already he got married with a girl from [?
Kalerne ?] and-- but he was in Torun, happened to be that time there.
So he-- we send him, and he went and-- and dropped this off to my father's sister, my-- our aunt.
And this happened about two or three weeks before August the 2nd, we did that.
When this officer told my father that, my older brother, Eliezer, his wife was a Hungarian.
He met her in the city of Debrecen and her mother was still alive.
So she decided-- they decided that she better-- it's too chokey up here.
We better go to Debrecen.
And they have-- they had two children.
The oldest one was about that time, about-- in-- in-- in 1940, '41, he was around 12 or 11, the oldest one.
The other one was around seven, eight.
And she went with-- with her-- the children to her mother in Debrecen.
The other brother, the-- the one that I'm talking about, Tsvi, his wife was in Kalerne or-- and they already had a baby.
The baby was only about a year old at that time.
So she also went away from Torun to-- to her parents.
The only one that-- that didn't go away was my brother Moshe, because his wife was from Torun so-- so where were-- where were they gonna go?
We're coming to the end of the tape now.
Oh, all right.
So I'll continue.
I'll continue on the next tape.
